text: Determining the socio-economic consequences of road accidents is one of the basic tasks in the field of road safety and is strictly defined by EU directives and national road safety strategies. Unfortunately, the accuracy of the assessment is very different in individual EU countries, due to, among other things, different levels of data availability and different methods used for their determination. This article attempts to assess the assumptions that underpin the methods of determining the unit value of road accidents and provides an overview of the basic concepts of costs: direct economic costs, indirect economic costs and the value of safety. Special attention is paid to the valuation of human life and non-material injuries, such as injury, illness or stigma resulting from an accident. An overview of the most commonly used methods for valuation, including: human capital method, hedonistic pricing and willingness-to-pay (WTP) methods, is also presented. The court awards for damage to life and health, as well as the loss of joy of life are also shown as important factors in determining the costs of road accidents in Poland and internationally. In conclusion, the article bridges the field of economic science and social policy, showing that an exact valuation of human life is an econometric necessity in relation to the evaluation of investments in infrastructure objects, and is also a very important tool for improving the knowledge of road accidents among the public.
